summary: Matrix recording with a solid soundboard‑plus‑audience mix captures one of the strongest 1987 shows – smoking Shakedown, monster He's Gone and a fire‑filled second set.
review: The show is repeatedly praised as a top‑tier ’87 performance, highlighted by Jerry Garcia’s searing leads on Shakedown and He’s Gone, Brent Mydland’s standout piano solo on Iko, and a rolling second‑set medley (Truckin' → Other One → Wharf Rat → Throwing Stones → NFA).  The matrix source blends the clarity of a soundboard with crowd ambience, delivering good‑to‑excellent fidelity despite occasional minor dropouts.

ai_rating.rationale: Over twenty reviews uniformly rate the show 4–5 stars and cite multiple standout songs; recording quality is consistently described as good/excellent, giving a high combined score.

recording_quality.technical_notes: Soundboard mixed with audience crowd, generally clear and powerful; a few reviewers note minor drop‑outs or a missing segment of The Other One but overall the mix is praised as crisp and well balanced.

show_quality.setlist_flow: First set opens with a thunderous Shakedown and tight mid‑tempo numbers; second set builds momentum through Iko, Saint of Circumstance and He’s Gone before launching into a classic Truckin' → Other One → Wharf Rat → Throwing Stones → NFA sequence, ending with a heartfelt Baby Blue.

band_member_comments.Jerry: Guitar described as ‘crankin’, ‘smoking’, ‘ABLAZE’, and even ‘angry post‑space’ – universally praised for intensity.
band_member_comments.Phil: Vocals noted as warm by some but overall vocal quality considered a bit weak on this night; bass playing not specifically highlighted.
band_member_comments.Keys: Brent Mydland’s piano solo on Iko and his work on Good Time Blues receive high praise, called ‘talented’ and a key driver of the show’s energy.
band_member_comments.Drums: Drummers get credit for solid support; one reviewer calls the drumming “bland routine” early but later notes the drums “mighty nice” during the jam sections.
